zones.name: Rex PRD Management CLI
zones.description: A self-contained CLI tool and MCP server for managing hierarchical product requirements documents, including code analysis, tree operations, validation, persistence, and AI-agent workflow integration.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 71% of project files (36/51) — may be too broad, consider splitting
The dependency graph flows cleanly from schema -> core -> store -> cli with no circular dependencies, indicating strong architectural discipline for a 36-file single-zone codebase.
The analyze module (scanners, propose, reconcile) is a well-isolated subsystem that could be extracted into its own zone if the project grows, since it has minimal coupling to core.
The MCP server in cli/mcp.ts at ~405 lines is the largest single file and serves as a second entry point alongside the CLI dispatcher, making it a natural seam for future zone splitting.

The store layer (file-adapter.ts) imports core/tree.js and core/canonical.js, creating a dependency inversion: store depends on core business logic instead of being a dumb persistence layer. Layering should be schema ← core ← store, but store reaches sideways into core.
PRIORITY_ORDER is duplicated three times (core/canonical.ts, core/next-task.ts, analyze/propose.ts) — a hidden coupling via copy-paste that risks drift if priority tiers change.
CLI commands init.ts and validate.ts bypass the store abstraction by directly using node:fs/promises for config file I/O, undermining the adapter pattern.
The core layer has no barrel export (no src/core/index.ts), so consumers import from individual files (core/tree.js, core/next-task.js), making the public API surface implicit rather than explicit.
REX_DIR constant is duplicated: declared in cli/commands/constants.ts and re-declared in cli/mcp.ts, creating a sync risk for the two entry points.

sortByPriority logic is duplicated between core/canonical.ts:14-27 (sortItems, recursive) and core/next-task.ts:11-18 (sortByPriority, shallow) — two subtly different sort implementations within the same core layer that can diverge.

MCP tool handlers in cli/mcp.ts reimplement the same orchestration logic as CLI commands (e.g., get_next_task duplicates cmdNext, update_task_status duplicates cmdUpdate) rather than sharing a common service layer, creating a parallel maintenance burden across ~400 lines.

The PRDItem interface in schema/v1.ts:21 has an index signature `[key: string]: unknown` alongside explicit fields, which defeats TypeScript's excess-property checking and allows arbitrary data to pass type checks silently — the `.passthrough()` on the Zod schema compounds this by accepting unknown keys at runtime too.

readLog() at store/file-adapter.ts:105 casts each parsed JSON line as LogEntry without validation (`JSON.parse(line) as LogEntry`), while appendLog() validates via Zod before writing — asymmetric serialization boundary means corrupted log files can inject malformed entries into the system.

The analyze --accept flow in cli/commands/analyze.ts:125-169 calls store.addItem() sequentially in a loop (one file write per item) with no batching or transaction, so a failure mid-loop leaves the PRD in a partially-updated state with no rollback.

update.ts:6-12 hardcodes VALID_STATUSES and VALID_PRIORITIES as string Sets instead of deriving them from the Zod enums in schema/validate.ts (ItemStatusSchema, PrioritySchema), creating a second source of truth that will drift if enum values change.
callSourceVisionMCP in recommend.ts:33-78 uses raw child_process.spawn with callback-based stream handling, but never sets a timeout — a hung sourcevision process will block the CLI indefinitely.

The PRDItemSchema in validate.ts:18 is typed as `z.ZodType<Record<string, unknown>>` erasing the inferred Zod type, so validateDocument returns `Record<string, unknown>` forcing every callsite to cast `as PRDDocument` — the Zod schema is well-typed internally but the export discards the type information.

PRIORITY_ORDER is defined three times (canonical.ts:7, next-task.ts:4, propose.ts:25) with two different type signatures — propose.ts uses Record<string, number> (loose) while the other two use Record<Priority, number> (strict), creating a silent type-safety gap
The adapter field in RexConfig is dead configuration — all 8 createStore() call sites hardcode the literal string "file" instead of reading config.adapter, making alternative adapters unreachable

Log event names for the same semantic operation differ between CLI and MCP: update.ts:68 emits "item_updated" while mcp.ts:129 emits "status_changed" for status changes, with no shared event-name constants
init.ts and validate.ts both bypass the store abstraction layer, using raw fs readFile/writeFile directly, making them incompatible with any non-file adapter
[call graph] 695 internal calls, 151 outgoing, 477 incoming (cohesion: 0.82, coupling: 0.18)

zones.name: Root
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Clean separation of concerns with orchestration scripts that spawn CLI processes rather than importing libraries
Unified entry point pattern through cli.js provides consistent user experience across all n-dx tools
Configuration management centralized in config.js enables cross-package settings coordination
Perfect cohesion and zero coupling demonstrates excellent architectural boundaries between orchestration and execution layers
Command delegation architecture: cli.js dispatches to ci.js, config.js, and web.js without importing domain packages — zero library coupling maintains layer isolation
Mixed file extension pattern: uses .js for executable scripts (cli.js, config.js) but potentially inconsistent with TypeScript-first architecture
Consider standardizing orchestration scripts to .ts extensions for consistency with TypeScript-first architecture, especially since other packages use .ts for public APIs
[call graph] 152 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Web
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 45% of project files (144/322) — subdivided into 3 sub-zones
Serves as the integration hub that brings together rex, sourcevision, and hench through a unified dashboard
MCP server implementation provides standardized tool access for Claude Code integration across all domain packages
Gateway pattern in mcp-deps.ts properly isolates cross-package imports to a single, auditable module
Largest package (144 files) with perfect cohesion demonstrates successful aggregation of multiple domain concerns without architectural compromise
Acts as integration hub with controlled coupling: 21 outgoing calls through gateway pattern maintains architectural discipline while aggregating domain services
Controlled aggregation pattern: single gateway module (mcp-deps.ts) manages all cross-package imports — prevents leaky abstractions across 144 files
Size disproportion: contains 45% of total project files (144/322) suggesting potential over-aggregation of concerns
Gateway isolation success: mcp-deps.ts properly constrains cross-package coupling to 21 calls across entire 144-file zone
Zone size indicates potential violation of single responsibility - 144 files may aggregate too many distinct concerns under dashboard umbrella
Import extension inconsistency: public.ts uses .js extensions for local imports in TypeScript files, mixing compilation strategies
Standardize import extensions: either use .js consistently (Node.js ESM style) or .ts extensions throughout the zone for clarity
Web zone shows classic monolithic symptoms: oversized (144 files), god functions (CallGraphView), tight coupling (routes-rex.ts), and dead exports (6+ unused functions)
Extract viewer subsystem from web zone: move packages/web/src/viewer/* to dedicated packages/viewer with clean interface — reduces zone size and isolates UI concerns
[call graph] 1740 internal calls, 21 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 0.99, coupling: 0.01)
Size imbalance: largest zone has 144 files vs smallest with 4 — uneven decomposition
Zero cross-zone imports demonstrates exceptional architectural discipline and successful implementation of the four-tier dependency hierarchy
Perfect cohesion scores across all zones indicate well-designed package boundaries that group related functionality without mixing concerns
Clean separation between orchestration, execution, domain, and foundation layers enables independent development and testing of each package
Architecture successfully implements zero-circular-dependency design with clear tier separation from orchestration down to foundation
Gateway pattern enforcement: cross-package dependencies flow through single modules (ops.ts, mcp-deps.ts) — prevents architecture drift as packages scale
Hierarchical coupling discipline: higher layers call lower layers through gateways, lower layers have zero upward coupling — maintains dependency hierarchy integrity
Four-tier layered architecture with unidirectional flow: orchestration → execution → domain → foundation — no layer bypassing or circular dependencies across 1403 import edges
Interface-based zone coupling: dashboard-platform's 21 outgoing calls represent clean service composition rather than tight coupling — dependency injection through gateway modules
Test isolation gap: packages-sourcevision:test-suite → packages-sourcevision:analysis-engine coupling suggests test code may be accessing implementation details rather than public APIs
Gateway pattern enforcement success: only 2 zones show outgoing calls, both through documented gateway modules preventing architecture drift
Test coupling to implementation: packages-sourcevision test suite has direct calls to analysis engine internals rather than testing through public interface
Zone size imbalance: 36x difference between largest (144 files) and smallest (4 files) zones indicates uneven responsibility distribution
Import extension strategy inconsistency: analysis-engine uses .js extensions in .ts files (ESM-style) while other zones may use different patterns
Configuration hardcoding pattern: test file detection patterns embedded in production analyzers rather than externalized to config
Establish consistent import extension strategy across all zones: either use .js extensions everywhere (Node.js ESM) or .ts extensions (TypeScript-style)
Architecture is sound at the macro level (zero circular dependencies, clean tiers) but suffers from micro-level antipatterns (god functions, fan-in hotspots, zone size imbalance)
Dead export pattern is systemic: 15+ unused exports across web, hench, and validation modules indicate insufficient cleanup during refactoring cycles
Test architecture violates encapsulation: sourcevision tests directly call analysis engine internals rather than testing through public interface, creating implementation coupling
Systematic dead export accumulation across 4+ zones suggests missing cleanup automation — consider adding unused export detection to CI pipeline
Fan-in concentration risk: 3 modules (tree.ts, routes-rex.ts, CallGraphView) handle disproportionate call volume, creating single points of failure for maintenance
[call graph] 33 calls: "packages-sourcevision:tests" → "packages-sourcevision:cli"
[call graph] 21 calls: "web" → "packages-rex:rex-cli"

findings.text: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Size imbalance: largest zone has 144 files vs smallest with 4 — uneven decomposition
Architecture successfully implements zero-circular-dependency design with clear tier separation from orchestration down to foundation
Fan-in hotspot: packages/rex/src/core/tree.ts receives calls from 31 files — high-impact module, changes may have wide ripple effects
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 45% of project files (144/322) — subdivided into 3 sub-zones
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Four-tier layered architecture with unidirectional flow: orchestration → execution → domain → foundation — no layer bypassing or circular dependencies across 1403 import edges
Systematic dead export accumulation across 4+ zones suggests missing cleanup automation — consider adding unused export detection to CI pipeline
Interface-based zone coupling: dashboard-platform's 21 outgoing calls represent clean service composition rather than tight coupling — dependency injection through gateway modules
Tightly coupled modules: packages/web/src/server/routes-rex.ts and packages/web/src/server/types.ts — 129 cross-file calls (unidirectional). Consider extracting shared interface or merging
Test coupling to implementation: packages-sourcevision test suite has direct calls to analysis engine internals rather than testing through public interface
Zone size imbalance: 36x difference between largest (144 files) and smallest (4 files) zones indicates uneven responsibility distribution
Fan-in concentration risk: 3 modules (tree.ts, routes-rex.ts, CallGraphView) handle disproportionate call volume, creating single points of failure for maintenance
God function: CallGraphView in packages/web/src/viewer/views/call-graph.ts calls 67 unique functions — consider decomposing into smaller, focused functions
Establish consistent import extension strategy across all zones: either use .js extensions everywhere (Node.js ESM) or .ts extensions (TypeScript-style)
Hub function: walkTree in packages/rex/src/core/tree.ts is called from 22 files — changes here have wide impact, consider if responsibilities can be narrowed
6 potentially unused exports in packages/web/src/schema/validate.ts have no incoming calls: validateManifest, validateInventory, validateImports, validateZones, validateComponents (+1 more)
3 potentially unused exports in packages/web/src/viewer/components/data-display/health-gauge.ts have no incoming calls: HealthGauge, PatternBadge, MetricCard
3 potentially unused exports in packages/web/src/viewer/components/logos.ts have no incoming calls: NdxLogoPng, ProductLogoPng, BrandedHeader
2 potentially unused exports in packages/hench/src/store/suggestions.ts have no incoming calls: loadSuggestionHistoryAsync, saveSuggestionHistoryAsync
Potentially unused export: cmdConfig in packages/hench/src/cli/commands/config.ts has no incoming calls — verify if still needed
Decompose packages/rex/src/core/tree.ts: extract tree traversal (walkTree), tree validation, and tree transformation into separate modules to reduce fan-in from 31 to <15 per module
Extract viewer subsystem from web zone: move packages/web/src/viewer/* to dedicated packages/viewer with clean interface — reduces zone size and isolates UI concerns
